title: Design a Distributed Blocking/Denylist System
shortDescription: Design a distributed denylist system that blocks requests from specified entities (users, IP addresses, emails, etc.). The system should integrate with external APIs to fetch blocked entity lists, handle both IPv4 and IPv6 addresses where applicable, support region-specific blocking rules, and efficiently propagate updates across a global infrastructure. Requirements include high availability, low latency lookups, real-time updates across multiple nodes, and the ability to handle government-mandated blocking scenarios.

Were You Asked This?
Design a Distributed Blocking/Denylist System

Design a distributed denylist system that blocks requests from specified entities (users, IP addresses, emails, etc.). The system should integrate with external APIs to fetch blocked entity lists, handle both IPv4 and IPv6 addresses where applicable, support region-specific blocking rules, and efficiently propagate updates across a global infrastructure. Requirements include high availability, low latency lookups, real-time updates across multiple nodes, and the ability to handle government-mandated blocking scenarios.

Asked at:

Google

LinkedIn

A global IP blocking service is like the IP/CIDR rules you configure in Cloudflare WAF or AWS WAF, enforced at the very first hop worldwide. Product teams use it to ingest government-provided blocklists, curate and approve policy updates, and automatically enforce those rules across every edge location with near-zero added latency.

Interviewers ask this because it tests your ability to design edge enforcement, distribute configuration globally, handle IPv4/IPv6 and CIDR ranges correctly, and operate safely under regulatory constraints. The best answers balance ultra-low latency on the hot path with a robust control plane for reliable updates, rollout, rollback, auditability, and clear fail-open vs fail-closed strategies.

Common Functional Requirements

Most candidates end up covering this set of core functionalities

Users should be able to block requests from specified IPs, CIDR ranges, and IP versions (IPv4/IPv6) globally with minimal added latency across HTTP, gRPC, WebSocket, and streaming traffic.

Users should be able to define region-scoped policies and exceptions (e.g., country-specific rules and allowlists) with deterministic precedence.

Users should be able to ingest policy updates from multiple government sources, review and approve them with auditability and versioning, and propagate changes globally within configured SLAs (including immediate blocks when mandated).

Users should be able to monitor enforcement, roll out changes safely (canary, staged), and perform instant rollback or emergency kill switches while maintaining compliance evidence.

Common Deep Dives

Common follow-up questions interviewers like to ask for this question

How do you achieve sub-millisecond per-request IP block decisions at the edge without adding noticeable latency?

How do you propagate and validate policy updates globally with different SLAs and avoid partial rollout inconsistencies?

How do you handle CIDR ranges, IPv6 scale, overlapping rules, region-scoped policies, and explicit allowlists with a clear precedence model?

What are your failure modes and operational controls (fail-open vs fail-closed, staleness, outages), and how do you prove compliance?

Relevant Patterns

Relevant patterns that you should know for this question

Real-time Updates

Blocked IPs must propagate to thousands of edge nodes quickly and consistently. A real-time updates pattern with durable pub/sub and atomic snapshot swaps provides fast fan-out, ordering, validation, and instant rollback without putting a central dependency on the request path.

Read More
Scaling Reads

Every incoming request needs a read-only policy lookup. Keeping the compiled policy in memory at the edge and optimizing data structures for constant-time or log-time membership checks ensures ultra-low latency and high availability.

Read More
Multi-step Processes

Ingestion from multiple government sources, normalization, deduplication, signing, approval, and staged rollout is a multi-step workflow. Modeling it as a durable process with clear states reduces errors and simplifies audits and rollbacks.

Read More
Relevant Technologies

Relevant technologies that could be used to solve this question

API Gateway

Gateways are the natural enforcement point across protocols (HTTP/gRPC/WebSocket). A gateway plugin/filter can perform the in-memory IP check at the first hop, ensuring consistent policy enforcement without changing every backend service.

Read More
Kafka

Kafka provides durable, ordered pub/sub for distributing versioned, signed policy snapshots to all regions. It enables fan-out, replay for recovery, and controlled canary/staged rollouts with clear versioning semantics.

Read More
Redis

Redis offers fast in-memory storage for policy snapshots and supports atomic swaps with minimal overhead. It can back gateway plugins or regional edges when you need shared memory, counters, or lightweight coordination without adding hot-path latency.

Read More
Similar Problems to Practice

Related problems to practice for this question

Rate Limiter

Both systems make per-request, low-latency decisions at the edge based on shared configuration that must be propagated globally and updated safely without central hot-path calls.

Read
Practice
Distributed Cache

Both require pushing small but critical config datasets to many nodes worldwide, handling invalidation, versioning, and atomic updates to avoid inconsistencies and downtime.

Read
Practice
Red Flags to Avoid

Common mistakes that can sink candidates in an interview

Calling a central service or database on every request to check if an IP is blocked

This adds network latency, creates a global dependency, and becomes a single point of failure. Under load or partial outages, it can cause widespread latency spikes or downtime.

Treating the list as plain strings and ignoring CIDR, IPv6, overlaps, and source IP normalization

Real policies include ranges, IPv6, and conflicting sources. Failing to normalize the client IP (X-Forwarded-For/Forwarded, NAT, IPv6-mapped IPv4) or to define precedence causes false positives/negatives and regulatory non-compliance.

No robust update pipeline with signing, versioning, staged rollout, and rollback

Bad or partial updates will propagate globally without a way to recover quickly. Missing signatures and audits also undermine compliance and trust in the system.

Same question asked with below constraints:

1. Govt. provides an API to check if an IP is blocked or not.
2. This REST API supports checking with only one IP
3. Govt. allows 7 days to enforce dening requests to this IP.
4. Asked me to solve just for one datacenter for now.

My experience:
I initially proposed in-memory cache + persistent DB latest I understand that persistent DB is not needed here. So redesigned according. I think this was the red flag to me for Staff level.

Result:
I got the feedback:
1. I didn't applied "Production Mitigation Strategies" properly
2. Unnecessarly introduce both in-memory and persistent DB
3. Didn't talked about hot partitions
4. Good at System Design, but not at Staff level, can be considered for L5 role.

I was asked to design a system that decides whether to allow or block an IP based on a Govt. API as well. The issue though was that the interviewer was very chatty and kept saying things like "oh btw there is one more thing you need to ask me. I can't tell you what it is, but its important".

It kept rattling me and made it difficult to follow the interview structure properly. I am just worried that he may submit the feedback as saying that he had to give hints for me to arrive at the solution that would result in a down-level from Staff to Senior.

EDIT: Apparently, he was satisfied with my interview and gave me a hire for that one. Now on to the next rounds.

YearlingHarlequinMite643
Premium
• 8个月前

Functional Requirements
Block request by IP Globally  - must be denied at the edge before reaching backedns systems.
Support multiple government blocklists - Accept from different contries/agensces. Enforce scope (global or country specific)
Efficient update to blocklist - Support both real time updates and periodic batch updates. Apply deta changes and not full reloads
Audit & compliance - Store versioned copies of blocklist. Provide logs for enforcement decisions.

Non Functional Requirements.
Scale : Handle 100+M DAU and millions of request/sec.  Edge enformcence must be logaritichim time
Latency : Lookup overhead per request < 10ms. Propagation of new block entries - critical <5s.  Normal advisory  < 1 hours
Availability vs Consistency - Availability priortized .  Eventual consistency for updates but must converge.
Reliability & Durability : 99.99% succes sin enforcing blocks . 11 nines durability for audig logs.
Security : All lists mut be cryptographicaaly singed by trusted issuers.

1

Reply

meta.title: Design a Distributed Blocking/Denylist System
meta.description: Design a distributed denylist system that blocks requests from specified entities (users, IP addresses, emails, etc.). The system should integrate with external APIs to fetch blocked entity lists, handle both IPv4 and IPv6 addresses where applicable, support region-specific blocking rules, and efficiently propagate updates across a global infrastructure. Requirements include high availability, low latency lookups, real-time updates across multiple nodes, and the ability to handle government-mandated blocking scenarios.
